network.overall_strategy: The network integrates at least 10 independent prebiotic routes to racemic alanine from simple C1–C3 feedstocks (CO₂, H₂, HCN, NH₃, H₂S), organized across hydrothermal, surface, and biochemical environments. Five hub molecules—pyruvate, acetaldehyde, HCN, formaldehyde, and α-aminopropionitrile—create a densely interconnected bipartite graph. Hydrothermal reactions provide carbon-fixed intermediates (formate, CO, formaldehyde, acetaldehyde, pyruvate) via mineral-catalyzed reductions on greigite, magnetite, mackinawite, and Fe/Ni nanoparticles. These intermediates flow to surface environments where Strecker, cyanosulfidic, Bucherer-Bergs, and HCN oligomerization chemistry operates under UV, wet-dry cycling, and moderate temperatures with clay, borate, phosphate, and sulfide mineral catalysts. Biochemical-stage reactions (serine decarboxylation, proto-metabolic transamination) complete the network. All known prebiotic routes produce racemic DL-alanine; the literature consensus (confirmed by Bennu/Ryugu data, Glavin et al. 2025) is that homochirality must arise terrestrially via downstream amplification mechanisms (e.g., CISS, crystallization, RNA-mediated selection), not during alanine synthesis itself.

reactions.name: CO₂ hydrogenation to formate on greigite

conditions.other: alkaline hydrothermal vent, pH 9-11, H₂ from serpentinization

reactions.mechanism: CO₂ adsorbs on greigite surface [4Fe-4S] cluster analog. H₂ dissociates on Fe sites providing hydride equivalents. Two-electron reduction of CO₂ to formate via surface-bound formate intermediate. Greigite is structurally analogous to biological [Fe₄S₄] cubane clusters in carbon-fixing enzymes.
reactions.reasoning: Russell et al. proposed greigite as primordial CO₂ reduction catalyst. Experimental CO₂ reduction to formate on Fe₃S₄ surfaces demonstrated under alkaline hydrothermal conditions. Greigite's structural homology to biological [4Fe-4S] clusters supports its role as a prebiotic catalyst (Lane and Martin, 2012, Cell 151:1406). Herschy et al. (2014, J. Mol. Evol.) demonstrated formaldehyde production from H₂/CO₂ using Fe(Ni)S catalysts at vent conditions.

reactions.name: CO₂ partial reduction to CO on magnetite

conditions.other: black smoker proximal zone, reverse water-gas shift

reactions.mechanism: Reverse water-gas shift reaction: CO₂ + H₂ → CO + H₂O catalyzed on magnetite surfaces. Fe⁰ acts as strong reductant, oxidizing to Fe²⁺/Fe³⁺ while reducing CO₂. At high temperatures the equilibrium favors CO production.
reactions.reasoning: Fischer-Tropsch-type (FTT) chemistry at hydrothermal vents begins with CO production from CO₂. Magnetite is a well-established FTT catalyst. The reverse water-gas shift provides CO feedstock for subsequent hydrogenation reactions. This reaction is thermodynamically favorable under hydrothermal conditions (Amend and Shock, 1998, Science 281:1659).

reactions.name: CO₂ reduction to pyruvate on Fe/Ni nanoparticles

conditions.other: alkaline hydrothermal conditions, pH 8-10, serpentinization-derived H₂

reactions.mechanism: Stepwise CO₂ reduction on Fe/Ni nanoparticle surfaces via formate and acetate intermediates to pyruvate. Ni sites facilitate C-C bond formation through CO insertion chemistry analogous to the acetyl-CoA pathway. The mixed Fe/Ni surface provides both reductive power and catalytic C-C coupling.
reactions.reasoning: Beyazay et al. (2023, Nature Communications 14:570) demonstrated that native iron and Fe/Ni alloy nanoparticles reduce CO₂ to pyruvate, acetate, and formate under hydrothermal conditions. This parallels the biological Wood-Ljungdahl pathway. Muchowska, Varma, and Moran (2019, Nature 569:104) showed iron-promoted reverse TCA cycle chemistry producing pyruvate from simple C1 inputs. Pyruvate has also been detected in carbonaceous meteorites (Cooper et al., 2011, PNAS 108:14015).

reactions.name: Formate reduction to formaldehyde on mackinawite

conditions.other: alkaline vent interior, pH 9-11, H₂-rich fluids

reactions.mechanism: Two-electron reduction of formate to formaldehyde on Ni-doped FeS (mackinawite) surfaces. The Ni dopant facilitates hydride transfer. Formaldehyde is released into solution but may be stabilized by minerals or rapid downstream reactions. At vent conditions, steady-state formaldehyde concentrations are maintained by continuous production.
reactions.reasoning: Herschy et al. (2014, J. Mol. Evol.) demonstrated formaldehyde production from H₂ and CO₂ using Fe(Ni)S catalysts, with formate as the likely intermediate. Pinto et al. estimated prebiotic ocean formaldehyde at ~10⁻³ M, and hydrothermal formate reduction is one plausible source. Mackinawite is the precursor to greigite and abundant at alkaline hydrothermal vents.

reactions.name: Fischer-Tropsch-type synthesis of formaldehyde

conditions.other: Fischer-Tropsch conditions, CO/H₂ ratio ~1:2

reactions.mechanism: CO adsorbs on magnetite surface, undergoes stepwise hydrogenation: CO → HCO (formyl) → H₂CO (formaldehyde). This is the first step of the classic Fischer-Tropsch-type mechanism. Formaldehyde is the C1 oxygenate product before chain growth to higher homologues.
reactions.reasoning: Fischer-Tropsch-type synthesis on magnetite is well-established in prebiotic chemistry. CO hydrogenation to formaldehyde, methanol, and higher products proceeds on Fe₃O₄ surfaces at 400-500K under high pressure (reviewed in McCollom, 2013). This provides an independent hydrothermal route to formaldehyde distinct from the formate reduction pathway (rxn_004).

reactions.name: Fischer-Tropsch-type synthesis of acetaldehyde on Fe/Ni sulfides

conditions.other: 80-120°C, 10-day timescale, CO/H₂ in hydrothermal fluid

reactions.mechanism: CO undergoes C-C coupling on mixed Ni,Fe sulfide surfaces via surface-bound methyl and formyl intermediates. The Ni sites promote CO insertion into surface-bound C₁ species, forming a C₂ intermediate that is hydrogenated and released as acetaldehyde. This is a multi-step surface-catalyzed process involving sequential CO adsorption, hydrogenation, chain growth, and product desorption.
reactions.reasoning: Huber and Wächtershäuser (2006, Science 314:630) demonstrated that CO and H₂ react over Fe/Ni mineral surfaces at 80-120°C to produce α-amino acids. The mechanism proceeds through aldehydes as intermediates; acetaldehyde is a key C₂ product of FTT synthesis. Unlike the earlier single-step claim, this is understood as a multi-step surface process: CO → formyl → hydroxymethyl → methyl → C-C coupling → acetaldehyde. Acetaldehyde has been detected in the interstellar medium in hot molecular cores, confirming its cosmochemical relevance.

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic photoreductive synthesis of acetaldehyde from formaldehyde

conditions.catalyst: Cu(I)/Cu(II) photoredox cycling on sulfide minerals
conditions.other: UV irradiation (λ < 300 nm), shallow pools, anoxic atmosphere, pH ~7

reactions.mechanism: Reductive homologation of HCN proceeds via Cu(I)-mediated single-electron reduction under UV. HCN is reduced to form an iminyl radical, which adds to formaldehyde. Subsequent hydrolysis and tautomerization yield glycolaldehyde, which undergoes further photochemical reduction and retro-aldol/rearrangement steps to produce acetaldehyde. H₂S serves as the terminal electron donor, being oxidized to elemental sulfur. The Cu(I)/Cu(II) cycle provides the photoredox driving force on sulfide mineral surfaces.
reactions.reasoning: Ritson and Sutherland (2012, 2013, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 52:5845) demonstrated synthesis of glycolaldehyde and acetaldehyde by photoredox chemistry from HCN and formaldehyde using Cu(I)/Cu(II) cycling and H₂S as reductant. This is a key step in the Sutherland cyanosulfidic systems chemistry approach that generates aldehyde precursors for downstream amino acid, nucleotide, and lipid synthesis (Patel et al., 2015, Nature Chemistry 7:301).

reactions.name: Imine formation from acetaldehyde and ammonia

conditions.other: mild aqueous conditions, pH 7-9, evaporitic pool

reactions.mechanism: Nucleophilic addition of ammonia nitrogen to the carbonyl carbon of acetaldehyde forms a hemiaminal (carbinolamine) intermediate, which undergoes dehydration to yield the imine (Schiff base) CH₃CH=NH. The reaction is reversible and favored at moderate pH where NH₃ is unprotonated. Montmorillonite clay surfaces concentrate both reactants via adsorption, shifting equilibrium toward product.
reactions.reasoning: Imine formation is the first step of the Strecker synthesis and is well-established under mild aqueous prebiotic conditions. Miller (1955, J. Am. Chem. Soc. 77:2351) confirmed this as the operative mechanism in spark discharge experiments. Clay minerals concentrate dilute organics from solution (Bernal, 1949 hypothesis), addressing the concentration problem (Stribling and Miller, 1987). The reaction is rapid and reversible at ambient temperature.

reactions.name: Strecker HCN addition to imine forming α-aminopropionitrile

conditions.other: pH 7-9, aqueous solution, HCN > 10⁻⁴ M

reactions.mechanism: Nucleophilic addition of cyanide (CN⁻) to the electrophilic carbon of the imine CH₃CH=NH. The CN⁻ attacks the sp² carbon, forming a new C-C bond and yielding α-aminopropionitrile (2-aminopropionitrile, CH₃CH(NH₂)CN). The reaction proceeds through a cyclic transition state with a barrier of ~9.6-21.3 kcal/mol (computationally determined for ice surface conditions).
reactions.reasoning: This is the defining step of the Strecker synthesis, first demonstrated by Strecker (1850) and confirmed as the prebiotic mechanism by Miller (1955, J. Am. Chem. Soc. 77:2351; Miller and Van Trump, 1981). The nitrogen isotope fractionation between α-aminopropionitrile amine nitrogen and free ammonia is 55.1‰ (Chimiak et al., 2022, Geochim. Cosmochim. Acta 321:78-98), which matches Murchison meteorite alanine, confirming Strecker as the dominant meteoritic synthesis pathway. Computational studies show ice surface protons enable proton-relay mechanisms lowering barriers.

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic phosphoro-Strecker synthesis of α-aminopropionitrile

conditions.other: UV irradiation, neutral pH, cyanosulfidic conditions, ~70% yield from acetaldehyde

reactions.mechanism: Under phosphate-buffered cyanosulfidic conditions, acetaldehyde reacts with ammonia and HCN via a phosphoro-Strecker variant. Phosphate acts as a general acid-base catalyst, facilitating imine formation and cyanide addition in a concerted or quasi-concerted fashion. The phosphate buffering prevents pH drift that would otherwise disfavor the reaction. Cu(I)/Cu(II) cycling maintains redox homeostasis. Yield of α-aminopropionitrile from acetaldehyde reaches approximately 70%.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015, Nature Chemistry 7:301) demonstrated that under phosphoro-Strecker conditions within the cyanosulfidic systems chemistry framework, α-aminopropionitrile forms at ~70% yield from acetaldehyde at neutral pH and 35°C. This is a key finding showing that amino acid, nucleotide, and lipid precursors can form simultaneously from the same feedstock under unified conditions. This route provides a second independent pathway to α-APN distinct from the classic two-step Strecker (rxn_008 + rxn_009).

reactions.name: α-Aminopropionitrile hydrolysis to alaninamide

conditions.catalyst: montmorillonite surface catalysis, mild acid or base
conditions.other: pH 5-9, aqueous, wet-dry cycling may concentrate products

reactions.mechanism: Nucleophilic addition of water to the nitrile group (C≡N) of α-aminopropionitrile. The nitrile carbon is attacked by water, proceeding through a tetrahedral intermediate to form the primary amide (alaninamide, CH₃CH(NH₂)CONH₂). This is the first of two sequential hydrolysis steps. The reaction is slow under neutral conditions but catalyzed by mineral surfaces and by mild acid/base.
reactions.reasoning: Nitrile hydrolysis to amide is the established second step of the Strecker pathway (Miller and Van Trump, 1981). Under prebiotic conditions, this step is rate-limiting relative to imine/HCN addition. Clay mineral surfaces can catalyze hydrolysis through surface acid-base chemistry. The amide intermediate (alaninamide) has been detected in Miller-Urey-type experiments and in hydrolysates of HCN oligomers.

reactions.name: Alaninamide hydrolysis to DL-alanine

conditions.other: pH 4-8, aqueous, evaporitic pool conditions

reactions.mechanism: Nucleophilic attack of water on the amide carbonyl carbon of alaninamide, proceeding through a tetrahedral intermediate. Elimination of NH₃ yields DL-alanine (2-aminopropanoic acid). The reaction produces racemic alanine because no chiral influence is present. Under mildly acidic conditions, the amide is protonated, activating it toward hydrolysis.
reactions.reasoning: The amide-to-acid hydrolysis completes the Strecker synthesis. Miller (1955, J. Am. Chem. Soc. 77:2351) confirmed this pathway as operative in spark discharge experiments. All Strecker routes produce racemic DL-alanine. This product is the second most abundant amino acid (after glycine) in Miller-type experiments at ~0.75% of input carbon (Miller, 1953, Science 117:528). Johnson et al. (2008, Science 322:404) found 22 amino acids upon reanalysis of Miller's archived vials.

reactions.name: Bucherer-Bergs CO₂ capture forming 5-methylhydantoin from α-APN

conditions.other: pH 7-9, CO₂ from atmosphere or decarboxylation, warm evaporitic pool

reactions.mechanism: CO₂ inserts into the amine N-H bond of α-aminopropionitrile to form a carbamate intermediate. Intramolecular cyclization then occurs as the carbamate nitrogen attacks the nitrile carbon, forming the five-membered hydantoin ring (5-methylhydantoin). This captures CO₂ that may be released from concomitant pyruvate decarboxylation, creating an elegant chemical coupling between amino acid and pyrimidine precursor synthesis.
reactions.reasoning: Pulletikurti, Yadav, Springsteen, and Krishnamurthy (2022, Nature Chemistry) demonstrated the Bucherer-Bergs pathway: pyruvate reacts with cyanide and ammonia, the aminonitrile intermediate captures CO₂ to form 5-methylhydantoin, which hydrolyzes to alanine. This pathway simultaneously produces dihydroorotate (a pyrimidine precursor), elegantly bridging amino acid and nucleobase synthesis. The hydantoin intermediate is more stable than the aminonitrile, providing a kinetic trap.

reactions.name: 5-Methylhydantoin hydrolysis to N-carbamoyl-DL-alanine

conditions.other: pH 7-9, prolonged heating at 80°C in evaporitic pool

reactions.mechanism: Hydrolytic ring-opening of the 5-methylhydantoin. Water attacks the C2 carbonyl of the hydantoin ring, cleaving the C-N bond to yield the open-chain N-carbamoyl amino acid (N-carbamoyl-DL-alanine). The reaction is slow at ambient temperature but proceeds at 80°C over hours to days.
reactions.reasoning: Pulletikurti et al. (2022, Nature Chemistry) showed that 5-methylhydantoin hydrolyzes to N-carbamoyl-DL-alanine at 80°C. This step is consistent with warm pool/hot spring environments where sustained elevated temperatures are available. The N-carbamoyl intermediate is prebiotically relevant as a protected amino acid form.

reactions.name: N-Carbamoyl-DL-alanine decarbamoylation to DL-alanine

conditions.other: pH 5-7, warm evaporitic pool, wet-dry cycling

reactions.mechanism: Hydrolytic decarbamoylation: the carbamoyl (ureido) group is cleaved by water, releasing CO₂ and NH₃, and yielding the free amino acid DL-alanine. The reaction is acid-catalyzed and proceeds at moderate temperatures. The released CO₂ may re-enter the Bucherer-Bergs cycle.
reactions.reasoning: Pulletikurti et al. (2022, Nature Chemistry) demonstrated the complete Bucherer-Bergs pathway from aminonitrile through hydantoin and N-carbamoyl intermediate to free DL-alanine at 80°C. This provides an alternative to direct aminonitrile hydrolysis (rxn_011 → rxn_012), proceeding through more stable intermediates. The recycling of CO₂ and NH₃ products creates a partly closed chemical cycle.

reactions.name: Reductive amination of pyruvate on iron oxyhydroxides

conditions.other: pH 9, alkaline hydrothermal vent sediment, anoxic

reactions.mechanism: Pyruvate reacts with NH₃ to form an imine (Schiff base) at the α-keto position. The Fe(II) component of the mixed-valence oxyhydroxide provides two electrons for reduction of the imine C=N bond, yielding DL-alanine. Simultaneously, Fe(II) is oxidized to Fe(III). The maximum yield occurs at a 1:1 Fe(II):Fe(III) ratio, suggesting that the mineral's mixed-valence character is essential for catalytic activity. This parallels the biological alanine transaminase reaction.
reactions.reasoning: Barge, Flores, Baum, VanderVelde, and Russell (2019, PNAS 116:4828) demonstrated pyruvate-to-alanine conversion on mixed-valence iron oxyhydroxides under alkaline conditions. Maximum yield at 1:1 Fe(II):Fe(III) is consistent with sediments near alkaline hydrothermal vents where serpentinization produces reduced iron. This directly parallels the modern biosynthetic pathway (alanine transaminase: pyruvate + NH₃ → alanine), supporting the metabolism-first hypothesis.

reactions.name: Ni-catalyzed reductive amination of pyruvate with H₂

conditions.other: pH 7-8, 30 mM pyruvate, NH₄⁺ in solution, serpentinization-derived H₂

reactions.mechanism: Metallic nickel surface dissociatively adsorbs H₂, generating surface-bound hydrides. Pyruvate reacts with NH₄⁺/NH₃ to form the Schiff base (imine). The nickel surface hydrides transfer to the imine, reducing the C=N bond to C-NH₂ and yielding DL-alanine. This closely parallels enzymatic alanine dehydrogenase using NADH as hydride donor, with Ni-H replacing the biological cofactor.
reactions.reasoning: Kaur, Rauscher, and Werner (Moran group, 2024, Chem 10:1528) demonstrated that nickel catalysts enable H₂ gas at 5 bar to drive reductive amination of 30 mM pyruvate to alanine at pH 7-8 and 22°C. This closely parallels enzymatic biosynthesis and operates under mild conditions achievable in serpentinizing hydrothermal settings where native Ni (awaruite) and H₂ are abundant. The low temperature requirement makes this compatible with cool regions of alkaline vents rather than high-temperature black smokers.

reactions.name: HCN oligomerization to polymer

conditions.other: HCN concentration > 0.01 M achieved by eutectic freezing at -23°C (25 M HCN at eutectic), pH > 9

reactions.mechanism: HCN undergoes base-catalyzed oligomerization: HCN → dimer (iminoacetonitrile) → trimer (aminomalononitrile) → tetramer (diaminomaleonitrile, DAMN) → higher oligomers and black polymer. The polymerization is initiated by CN⁻ (the base form of HCN) attacking another HCN molecule. At concentrations above 0.01 M, extensive polymerization yields a heterogeneous black polymer containing trapped amino acid and nucleobase precursors within its conjugated backbone.
reactions.reasoning: HCN oligomerization was first demonstrated by Oró (1961) and extensively studied by Ferris et al. (1978, J. Mol. Evol. 11:293). The critical concentration threshold of ~0.01 M is achievable through eutectic freezing at -23°C, which concentrates HCN to ~25 M (Miyakawa, Cleaves, and Miller, 2002, Orig. Life Evol. Biosph. 32:195). At steady state in a warm ocean at pH 8, HCN is only ~7×10⁻¹³ M — far too dilute — making concentration mechanisms essential. Clay surfaces provide additional concentration and catalysis.

reactions.name: HCN polymer hydrolysis to DL-alanine

conditions.catalyst: acid hydrolysis (HCl or mineral acid), montmorillonite
conditions.other: pH 1-3 for acid hydrolysis, or pH 10+ for base hydrolysis, 6-24 hours at ~100°C

reactions.mechanism: Acid or base hydrolysis of the HCN polymer backbone cleaves amide and nitrile bonds, releasing a mixture of amino acids including glycine, alanine, aspartate, and others. The polymer likely contains embedded aminonitrile and amide units that liberate free amino acids upon hydrolysis. Alanine is detected among the products in most conditions, though as a minor component relative to glycine.
reactions.reasoning: Oró (1961) and Ferris et al. (1978, J. Mol. Evol. 11:293) showed that HCN oligomer hydrolysis releases multiple amino acids including alanine. Prebiotically, acid conditions could arise from volcanic HCl outgassing into shallow pools, or base conditions from alkaline fluid discharge. This pathway is significant because it produces amino acids from a single feedstock (HCN) without requiring separate aldehyde or ammonia sources.

reactions.name: Thermal decarboxylation of serine to DL-alanine

conditions.catalyst: pyrite surface, high pressure prevents boiling
conditions.other: subseafloor hydrothermal fluid, 20-100 min residence time

reactions.mechanism: β-Elimination and decarboxylation of serine: the hydroxyl group on the β-carbon facilitates loss of CO₂ (as formaldehyde or directly) from the carboxyl group, yielding alanine. At hydrothermal temperatures (200-250°C), serine rapidly decomposes; alanine is a major decarboxylation product. The net reaction is effectively the loss of the hydroxymethyl side chain as formaldehyde or CO₂/H₂, depending on conditions. Alanine itself is more thermally stable, showing relatively little decomposition over 20-100 minutes at these temperatures.
reactions.reasoning: Chandru et al. (2013, Orig. Life Evol. Biosph. 43:99) demonstrated that at 200-250°C, serine and aspartate rapidly decarboxylate, with alanine as a major product. Critically, alanine shows relatively little decomposition at these temperatures over 20-100 min, making it a kinetic sink that accumulates as other amino acids decompose. This effectively enriches alanine relative to other amino acids in hydrothermal settings, providing an enrichment mechanism analogous to natural selection among small molecules.

reactions.name: Formose reaction: formaldehyde to glycolaldehyde

conditions.catalyst: Ca(OH)₂ or borate minerals, montmorillonite surface
conditions.other: pH 10-12, alkaline evaporitic pool, warm conditions

reactions.mechanism: Autocatalytic aldol condensation of formaldehyde. The reaction is initiated by traces of glycolaldehyde (or other C₂+ sugars) that act as the autocatalytic seed. Formaldehyde undergoes base-catalyzed enolization, and the resulting carbanion attacks another formaldehyde molecule. Borate minerals stabilize sugar intermediates by forming cyclic borate esters, preventing degradation and channeling the reaction toward glycolaldehyde rather than a complex mixture (the 'tar problem').
reactions.reasoning: The formose reaction is the classic prebiotic route to sugars from formaldehyde (Butlerow, 1861). Borate minerals stabilize ribose and glycolaldehyde (Ricardo et al., 2004, Science 303:196), making this reaction prebiotically viable in boron-rich volcanic hot spring environments. Glycolaldehyde serves as a precursor for both sugar chemistry (ribose synthesis) and Strecker synthesis of serine (via glycolaldehyde + NH₃ + HCN).

reactions.name: Strecker synthesis of serine from glycolaldehyde

conditions.other: pH 7-9, aqueous evaporitic pool, HCN and NH₃ available

reactions.mechanism: Glycolaldehyde (HOCH₂CHO) reacts with NH₃ to form an imine, which undergoes HCN addition to form the α-aminonitrile of serine (2-amino-3-hydroxypropionitrile). Sequential hydrolysis of the nitrile to amide and then carboxylic acid yields DL-serine. This is the standard Strecker pathway applied to a hydroxy-aldehyde substrate.
reactions.reasoning: Strecker synthesis from glycolaldehyde is the established prebiotic route to serine (Miller and Van Trump, 1981). Glycolaldehyde is produced by the formose reaction (rxn_021) and by cyanosulfidic photochemistry. Serine is important in this network because it decarboxylates to alanine under hydrothermal conditions (rxn_020, Chandru et al., 2013), providing an indirect route to alanine via a C₃ hydroxy amino acid. This pathway demonstrates environmental interconnection: Surface synthesis of serine → Hydrothermal decomposition to alanine.

reactions.name: Pyruvate cyanoamination to α-aminopropionitrile (Bucherer-Bergs entry)

conditions.other: pH 7-9, pyruvate from hydrothermal source, HCN and NH₃ in solution

reactions.mechanism: Pyruvate undergoes nucleophilic addition by cyanide to form a cyanohydrin at the α-keto position, yielding 2-cyano-2-hydroxypropanoate. Ammonia then displaces the hydroxyl group via SN2 or elimination-addition, forming α-aminopropionitrile. Alternatively, pyruvate first forms a Schiff base with NH₃, followed by cyanide addition. Both pathways converge on α-APN, which enters the Bucherer-Bergs cycle (rxn_013) or Strecker hydrolysis (rxn_011).
reactions.reasoning: Pulletikurti et al. (2022, Nature Chemistry) showed that pyruvate reacts with cyanide and ammonia to form the α-aminopropionitrile intermediate that then enters the Bucherer-Bergs hydantoin pathway. This provides a critical third route to α-APN (alongside rxn_009 and rxn_010), establishing it as a true hub molecule. The pyruvate starting material comes from hydrothermal CO₂ reduction (rxn_003), creating an important Hydrothermal → Surface environmental interconnection.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal formate → formaldehyde → cyanosulfidic acetaldehyde → classic Strecker
pathways.description: CO₂ is reduced to formate on greigite in alkaline hydrothermal vents, then to formaldehyde on mackinawite. Formaldehyde is transported to surface pools where cyanosulfidic photoredox chemistry converts it to acetaldehyde. Classic two-step Strecker synthesis (imine formation then HCN addition) yields α-aminopropionitrile, which hydrolyzes to alaninamide and then DL-alanine.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic phosphoro-Strecker from formaldehyde
pathways.description: Formaldehyde (from hydrothermal sources) undergoes cyanosulfidic photoreductive homologation to acetaldehyde in surface pools. The phosphoro-Strecker variant under Cu(I)/Cu(II) photoredox cycling directly converts acetaldehyde to α-aminopropionitrile at ~70% yield, which then hydrolyzes to DL-alanine.

pathways.name: Bucherer-Bergs via acetaldehyde-derived α-APN
pathways.description: Acetaldehyde (from Fischer-Tropsch synthesis) reacts via Strecker to form α-aminopropionitrile, which captures CO₂ to form 5-methylhydantoin (Bucherer-Bergs pathway). Sequential hydrolysis through N-carbamoyl-DL-alanine yields DL-alanine. This route simultaneously produces pyrimidine precursors.

pathways.name: Direct reductive amination of pyruvate on iron oxyhydroxides
pathways.description: CO₂ is directly reduced to pyruvate on Fe/Ni nanoparticles in hydrothermal vents. Pyruvate undergoes reductive amination on mixed-valence iron oxyhydroxides (green rust) at alkaline pH, directly yielding DL-alanine. This is the most direct route (fewest steps) and parallels the biological alanine transaminase reaction.

pathways.name: Ni-catalyzed reductive amination of pyruvate with H₂
pathways.description: Pyruvate (from hydrothermal CO₂ reduction) undergoes Ni-catalyzed reductive amination using serpentinization-derived H₂ at low temperature and moderate H₂ pressure. This closely parallels enzymatic alanine dehydrogenase and operates at near-ambient temperature.

pathways.name: HCN oligomerization and hydrolysis
pathways.description: HCN concentrates via eutectic freezing to >0.01 M and undergoes oligomerization to form a heterogeneous black polymer. Acid or base hydrolysis of the polymer releases a mixture of amino acids including DL-alanine. This single-feedstock pathway requires only HCN and water.

pathways.name: Fischer-Tropsch acetaldehyde → classic Strecker → DL-alanine
pathways.description: CO₂ is partially reduced to CO on magnetite at high temperature. CO and H₂ undergo Fischer-Tropsch-type synthesis on Ni,Fe sulfide minerals to produce acetaldehyde as a C₂ product. Acetaldehyde is transported to surface pools where it enters the classic two-step Strecker pathway (imine + HCN → α-APN → alaninamide → DL-alanine).

pathways.name: Serine decarboxylation enrichment pathway
pathways.description: Formaldehyde is converted to glycolaldehyde via the formose reaction in borate-rich surface pools. Glycolaldehyde undergoes Strecker synthesis to produce DL-serine. When serine is transported to hydrothermal environments (200-250°C), it rapidly decarboxylates to DL-alanine, which is more thermally stable and accumulates. This demonstrates bidirectional environmental flow: Hydrothermal → Surface (formaldehyde) → Surface (serine synthesis) → Hydrothermal (decarboxylation to alanine).

pathways.name: Bucherer-Bergs via pyruvate cyanoamination
pathways.description: Pyruvate from hydrothermal CO₂ reduction flows to surface pools where it reacts with HCN and NH₃ to form α-aminopropionitrile via cyanoamination. The α-APN then enters the Bucherer-Bergs pathway through CO₂ capture, hydantoin formation, and sequential hydrolysis to DL-alanine. This route is distinct from P3 because the α-APN is derived from pyruvate rather than acetaldehyde.

pathways.name: Fischer-Tropsch formaldehyde → cyanosulfidic acetaldehyde → phosphoro-Strecker
pathways.description: CO from hydrothermal CO₂ reduction is hydrogenated to formaldehyde via Fischer-Tropsch-type synthesis on magnetite. Formaldehyde is transported to surface environments where cyanosulfidic photoredox chemistry with HCN and H₂S converts it to acetaldehyde. The phosphoro-Strecker variant then produces α-APN at high yield, followed by hydrolysis to DL-alanine. This pathway combines all three chemical traditions: iron-sulfur world (FTT), cyanosulfidic (Sutherland), and classic Strecker (Miller).

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Well-constructed 7-step pathway integrating hydrothermal CO₂ fixation to formate, mineral-catalyzed reduction to formaldehyde, cyanosulfidic photoreductive synthesis of acetaldehyde, and classic Strecker sequence. The formate-to-formaldehyde step on mackinawite (rxn_004) is the weakest link—less experimentally documented than other steps—but plausible. The hydrothermal→surface environmental transition is natural and well-justified. Literature alignment is strong with Ritson & Sutherland 2013 and Miller 1955.
suggestions: Provide more specific conditions for rxn_004 (temperature, pH, reductant); consider adding an alternative route from CO/H₂ to formaldehyde (rxn_005) as a backup entry to this pathway

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Cyanosulfidic phosphoro-Strecker route closely follows Patel et al. 2015 (Nature Chemistry). The one-pot α-APN formation at ~70% yield from acetaldehyde under phosphoro-Strecker conditions is well-supported. Pathway is shorter than P1 by bypassing the explicit imine step. Shares the rxn_004 weakness with P1. Overall a strong, literature-grounded route.
suggestions: Specify Cu(I)/Cu(II) photoredox cycling and phosphate buffer conditions for rxn_010 to better match Patel et al. 2015

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Bucherer-Bergs pathway via hydantoin intermediate is well-supported by Pulletikurti et al. 2022 (Nature Chemistry). The 5-methylhydantoin intermediate is chemically plausible and provides an alternative hydrolysis route avoiding direct aminonitrile hydrolysis. The decarbamoylation step (rxn_015) at 80°C is reasonable. Nice integration of CO₂ capture into the hydantoin ring. The pathway also elegantly links to pyrimidine synthesis per literature.
suggestions: Consider specifying the 80°C condition for rxn_015 explicitly; note the connection to dihydroorotate synthesis as per Pulletikurti et al.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Shortest pathway: direct CO₂ reduction to pyruvate (Beyazay et al. 2023) followed by reductive amination on iron oxyhydroxides (Barge et al. 2019). Both steps are experimentally demonstrated with specific mineral catalysts. The pathway is entirely hydrothermal, which means no environmental progression to surface, but this is chemically justified for alkaline vent chemistry. The 1:1 Fe(II):Fe(III) requirement for rxn_016 is well-documented. Yields of 0.1–5% are realistic.
suggestions: Specify the 1:1 Fe(II):Fe(III) ratio and pH 9 conditions for rxn_016 for full Barge et al. alignment

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Parallel to P4 but uses Ni catalysis with H₂ gas (Kaur et al. 2024, Chem). The 5 bar H₂ pressure at 22°C and pH 7–8 is somewhat unusual for a hydrothermal label—these are more moderate conditions. Nevertheless, both steps are experimentally validated. The pathway closely parallels modern enzymatic transamination, supporting the proto-metabolic hypothesis.
suggestions: Consider whether the 22°C/5 bar H₂ conditions are better labeled as 'Surface' or specify that these conditions can also occur in low-temperature hydrothermal settings

pathway_evaluations.feedback: HCN oligomerization and hydrolysis (Oró 1961, Ferris et al. 1978) is a well-known route but less selective—the polymer produces many amino acids and other products. Alanine yields from HCN polymer are modest and variable. The concentration requirement (>0.01 M HCN) is a significant constraint, though eutectic freezing can address this. The pathway is short and valid but less controlled than Strecker routes.
suggestions: Specify HCN concentration requirements and hydrolysis conditions (acid vs base); consider adding eutectic freezing as a concentration mechanism; note that this produces a complex mixture, not selective alanine synthesis

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Excellent pathway combining Fischer-Tropsch-type hydrothermal chemistry (Huber & Wächtershäuser 2006) with the classic Strecker sequence. The CO₂→CO→acetaldehyde hydrothermal entry followed by surface Strecker synthesis represents an ideal hydrothermal→surface environmental flow. All individual steps are well-supported experimentally. This is one of the strongest and most literature-aligned pathways in the network.
suggestions: Could specify Fe/Ni sulfide mineral types and temperature range (80–120°C) for FTT steps

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Serine decarboxylation pathway is well-supported by Chandru et al. 2013, who showed alanine enrichment at hydrothermal temperatures from serine decomposition. The formose reaction step (rxn_021) is known to be problematic without borate or other stabilizers—formaldehyde tends to produce complex sugar mixtures. The Strecker synthesis of serine from glycolaldehyde (rxn_022) is standard. Overall a viable but less efficient route that importantly captures the hydrothermal enrichment phenomenon.
suggestions: Specify borate mineral stabilization for formose reaction to favor glycolaldehyde over complex sugars; note that this pathway's main prebiotic relevance is the hydrothermal enrichment of alanine from other amino acids

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Combines hydrothermal pyruvate synthesis (Beyazay et al. 2023) with surface Bucherer-Bergs chemistry (Pulletikurti et al. 2022). The pyruvate cyanoamination step (rxn_023) to form α-APN is less directly documented than the acetaldehyde Strecker route but is mechanistically plausible. The pathway nicely bridges hydrothermal and surface environments with pyruvate as the bridge molecule.
suggestions: Provide more mechanistic detail for rxn_023—is this a reductive cyanation or a direct Strecker on the keto group? Specify conditions more precisely

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Strong pathway combining FTT formaldehyde synthesis with cyanosulfidic photochemistry and phosphoro-Strecker. Shows excellent environmental progression from hydrothermal CO₂ reduction through surface cyanosulfidic and Strecker chemistry. The sequence CO₂→CO→HCHO→CH₃CHO→α-APN→alaninamide→DL-Ala is chemically coherent and well-documented across multiple publications.
suggestions: This pathway effectively combines the best of hydrothermal and cyanosulfidic chemistry; specify UV wavelength range for cyanosulfidic step

evaluation.network_feedback: This is an outstanding prebiotic synthesis network for DL-alanine that demonstrates exceptional alignment with the current literature. The network successfully integrates 10 independent pathways covering all major experimentally validated routes: Strecker synthesis (Miller 1955), cyanosulfidic chemistry (Patel et al. 2015), reductive amination on iron oxyhydroxides (Barge et al. 2019) and Ni catalysts (Kaur et al. 2024), Bucherer-Bergs hydantoin route (Pulletikurti et al. 2022), HCN oligomerization (Oró 1961), FTT synthesis (Huber & Wächtershäuser 2006), serine decarboxylation (Chandru et al. 2013), and CO₂ reduction to pyruvate (Beyazay et al. 2023). The five hub molecules (HCN, formaldehyde, acetaldehyde, pyruvate, α-aminopropionitrile) are well-chosen and create a densely connected bipartite graph with appropriate degree distributions. Environmental progression from hydrothermal mineral-catalyzed reactions to surface UV/wet-dry cycling chemistry is well-implemented, with mineral catalysts (greigite, magnetite, mackinawite, Fe/Ni nanoparticles, iron oxyhydroxides) properly specified for hydrothermal reactions. The network correctly targets racemic DL-alanine and addresses the chirality question by referencing the Bennu/Ryugu paradigm shift (Glavin et al. 2025). Minor weaknesses include: (1) the formate→formaldehyde step on mackinawite needs more experimental validation, (2) the formose reaction step lacks specification of borate stabilizers, (3) the HCN polymer pathway is relatively uncontrolled, and (4) some reaction conditions could be more precisely specified. The network could be improved by adding the UV ice analog pathway (Bernstein et al. 2002) and eutectic freezing concentration mechanism. Overall, this is one of the most comprehensive and well-documented prebiotic synthesis networks for alanine.
